INTERACTIVE COURSE   MULTIPROTOCOL LABEL SWITCHING
// Networking · Service Provider Technologies

BASIC MPLS

Multiprotocol Label Switching fundamentals, label switching, Label Switched Paths, and the role of MPLS in service provider networks.

📅 04 June
01
// Introduction
What Is MPLS?

MPLS (Multiprotocol Label Switching) is a packet-forwarding technique that uses a short label instead of performing a full destination-IP lookup at every hop. Edge routers attach a label to each packet, while core routers simply swap labels — far faster than IP lookups. This enables efficient Traffic Engineering, QoS and VPN services.

ANIMATED — A Packet Crossing the MPLS Core
PUSH → SWAP → POP: the edge router (PE-1) pushes a label, core routers (P) swap it hop by hop, then PE-2 pops the label, leaving a plain IP packet.
Faster
Swap a label instead of a full IP-table lookup — high-speed forwarding through the core.
Flexible
Runs over any Layer 2 ("Multiprotocol") and supports Traffic Engineering.
Enables VPNs
The foundation of provider L3VPN / L2VPN services, isolating each customer’s traffic.
02
// Architecture
LER, LSR and LSP

An MPLS network is built from routers playing two main roles, connected along a predetermined path called an LSP (Label Switched Path).

LER — Label Edge Router
Sits at the network edge. The ingress LER classifies packets into a FEC and pushes a label; the egress LER pops the label before handing the packet to the customer.
LSR — Label Switch Router
A core router. It receives a labeled packet, looks the label up in the LFIB, swaps it for a new label, and forwards it — with no IP lookup needed.
FEC — Forwarding Equivalence Class
A group of packets forwarded the same way (assigned the same label and traveling the same LSP).
LSP — Label Switched Path
A unidirectional path from the ingress PE to the egress PE, shared by all packets in the same FEC.
CE / PE / P: CE = Customer Edge (customer side) · PE = Provider Edge (provider edge = LER) · P = Provider core (= LSR)
03
// Packet Format
MPLS Label Structure (32-bit)

An MPLS label is 32 bits long, inserted between the Layer 2 header and the IP header (hence "shim header"). It has four fields.

ANIMATED — Walking Through the Four Label Fields
Fields reveal slowly so you can follow along: Label (20b) → TC/EXP (3b) → S (1b) → TTL (8b)
FieldSizePurpose
Label20 bitThe label value looked up in the LFIB (up to ~1,048,575).
TC / EXP3 bitTraffic Class — used for QoS / priority.
S1 bitBottom of Stack — =1 marks the lowest label in the stack.
TTL8 bitTime To Live — prevents loops, like IP TTL.
Label Stack: Labels can be stacked several deep; the S bit marks the bottom one — heavily used in L3 VPN, which stacks two labels (see section 05).
04
// Control Plane
LDP — Label Distribution

Routers must agree on which label represents which prefix. LDP (Label Distribution Protocol) does this: neighbors exchange label-to-FEC bindings with each other.

ANIMATED — Two LSRs Exchanging Bindings
Steps: discover neighbors (Hello) → establish a session (Init/Open) → advertise label bindings (FEC → label).
LDP Steps
1. Discovery — send Hellos to find neighbors
2. Session — establish an LDP session (TCP)
3. Advertisement — distribute label bindings
4. Maintenance — keepalives sustain the session
LIB / LFIB
LIB stores every binding learned.
LFIB is the table actually used when forwarding (incoming label → outgoing label + interface).
05
// L3 VPN
MPLS Layer 3 VPN

MPLS’s flagship service is the L3 VPN, letting many customers share one network with fully isolated traffic. The core ingredients are VRF + RD + RT + MP-BGP plus a two-label stack.

RD — Route Distinguisher
Prepended to a customer prefix to make it unique, turning overlapping IPs (e.g. several customers all using 10.0.0.0/8) into unique VPNv4 routes.
RT — Route Target
Controls which VRFs import/export which routes — deciding who sees whose routes.
MP-BGP (VPNv4)
Carries customer routes, together with their RD/RT and VPN label, across the core between PEs.
Two-label stack
Outer label = transport (gets it to the egress PE); inner label = VPN (selects the VRF / destination).
PHP (Penultimate Hop Popping): the second-to-last router pops the transport label so the egress PE does less work, leaving only the VPN label for the PE to read and select the VRF.
06
// VRF
VRF — Per-Customer Routing Tables

VRF (Virtual Routing and Forwarding) is an independent virtual routing table per customer on the same PE, letting customers with overlapping IPs coexist without collisions.

ANIMATED — VRF Isolating Two Customers With the Same IP
Customer-A and Customer-B both use 10.0.0.0/8, but their RDs differ (100:1 vs 100:2), so they never mix.
Principle: each VRF has its own RIB/FIB and interface assignments — like several virtual routers inside one box.
07
// L2 VPN
MPLS Layer 2 VPN

Beyond L3 VPN, MPLS can also carry Layer 2 frames across the core via a pseudowire, making customer sites appear connected on the same LAN / circuit.

VPWS
Virtual Private Wire Service — a point-to-point pseudowire that emulates a leased line between two sites.
VPLS
Virtual Private LAN Service — multipoint LAN emulation, making every site appear to share one switch.
L2 vs L3 VPN: with L2VPN the provider forwards frames without touching the customer’s routing — the customer runs its own routing; with L3VPN the provider participates in routing via VRF/MP-BGP.
08
// Hands-on
Configuration (Cisco IOS)

Here is how the pieces come together on the CLI. The terminal below types out the three core tasks — enabling LDP on the core, building an L3 VPN (VRF + RD/RT + MP-BGP), and bringing up an L2 VPN pseudowire — slowly enough to follow each line.

ANIMATED — Typing the MPLS configuration line by line
Cisco IOS syntax. Replace interface names, loopback IDs and addresses to match your topology.
1 · Core — enable LDP
! enable MPLS + LDP mpls label protocol ldp mpls ldp router-id Lo0 force interface Gig0/0 ip address 10.1.1.1 255..252 mpls ip
2 · L3 VPN — VRF + BGP
! per-customer VRF ip vrf CUSTOMER-A rd 100:1 route-target both 100:1 interface Gig0/1 ip vrf forwarding CUSTOMER-A ip address 192.168.1.1 ... router bgp 65000 address-family vpnv4 neighbor 2.2.2.2 activate ... send-community extended
3 · L2 VPN — pseudowire
! point-to-point VC 100 interface Gig0/2 xconnect 2.2.2.2 100 \ encapsulation mpls ! verify show mpls l2transport vc show mpls ldp neighbor show ip route vrf CUSTOMER-A
Verify: show mpls ldp neighbor (LDP up?) · show mpls forwarding-table (labels assigned?) · show ip bgp vpnv4 all (VPN routes?) · show mpls l2transport vc (pseudowire up?)
Platform note: These commands are Cisco IOS / IOS-XE. IOS-XR and Junos use different syntax (e.g. IOS-XR builds VRFs under vrf with address-family stanzas; Junos uses routing-instances of type vrf).
